quiz.name: Session 08 Entry Quiz: Event Correlation and Normalization
quiz.intro: Assess prerequisite knowledge before the session. 5 multiple-choice questions. Select the single best answer.

questiontext.text: A Security Operations Center ingests logs from 12 different security tools. An analyst wants to run a query to find all authentication failures regardless of which tool generated the log. What is the primary obstacle to writing this single query?

answer.text: Authentication failures are too rare to query across all sources
Different tools use different field names for the same concepts (e.g., source IP, username)

feedback.text: Correct. Without normalization, each source uses its own naming conventions. Log normalization solves this by mapping all sources to a common schema.

answer.text: SIEM query languages cannot handle multiple log sources simultaneously
Authentication events always require real-time processing

questiontext.text: In the syslog format <165>1 2024-12-14T07:42:01Z host sshd 3822 - Failed password, what does the number 165 represent?

answer.text: The message ID
The syslog version
The priority value, encoding both facility and severity

feedback.text: Correct. The PRI field = (facility × 8) + severity. PRI 165 = facility 20 (local4) × 8 + 5 (notice). The PID is 3822.

answer.text: The process identifier (PID)

questiontext.text: Which of the following best describes the difference between parsing and normalization in log processing?

answer.text: Parsing is done at the source; normalization happens at the SIEM
Parsing extracts structured fields from raw text; normalization maps those fields to a standard schema

feedback.text: Correct. These are two distinct pipeline stages. Parsing gives you fields; normalization gives you standard field names.

answer.text: Parsing converts logs to JSON; normalization compresses them for storage
There is no meaningful difference — the terms are interchangeable

question.name: Q4: False Positives and TP Rate

questiontext.text: A SOC analyst notices that a detection rule generates 300 alerts per day, but only about 15 turn out to be actual attacks. What is this phenomenon called, and what is the true positive (TP) rate?

answer.text: False negative storm; TP rate is 95%
Alert fatigue caused by false positives; TP rate is 5%

feedback.text: Correct. TP rate = 15/300 = 5%. High false positive rates lead to alert fatigue, where analysts become desensitized and may miss real incidents.

answer.text: Threshold misconfiguration; TP rate is 15%
Normalization error; TP rate is 50%

questiontext.text: Which of the following best describes what a correlation rule does compared to a single-event detection rule?

answer.text: Correlation rules are faster to execute; single-event rules require more computation
Correlation rules analyze relationships between multiple events over time; single-event rules evaluate one event independently

feedback.text: Correct. Single-event rules evaluate each event in isolation. Correlation rules combine multiple events — counting failures over time, or detecting attack sequences.

answer.text: Correlation rules only work with Windows event logs; single-event rules work with any log source
Correlation rules require machine learning; single-event rules use regex
